title: LAMOL: LAnguage MOdeling for Lifelong Language Learning

structured.summary: LAMOL proposes a simple lifelong learning approach for NLP by leveraging a single pretrained language model to both solve tasks reformulated as question answering and generate pseudo-samples of previous tasks for rehearsal, mitigating catastrophic forgetting without storing real data or increasing model size. Evaluated on diverse NLP benchmarks, it achieves near multitask performance and outperforms prior lifelong learning baselines.

strengths.point: Innovative use of a single pretrained LM for both task solving and pseudo-sample generation
strengths.evidence: The method reformulates all NLP tasks as QA and uses the same LM to generate pseudo-samples, enabling rehearsal without extra memory or model components.

strengths.point: Strong empirical results across diverse NLP tasks
strengths.evidence: LAMOL reduces forgetting significantly compared to fine-tuning, EWC, MAS, and GEM, approaching multitask upper bounds on decaNLP and text classification.

strengths.point: Task-specific tokens improve stability and scalability
strengths.evidence: Introducing distinct generation tokens per previous task balances pseudo-sample generation and yields better performance as the number of tasks grows.
The authors provide code and resources, facilitating further research and adoption.

weaknesses.point: Missing comparisons to recent generative replay methods in NLP
weaknesses.evidence: The paper lacks baselines using transformer-based generative replay or GPT-style replay frameworks relevant to lifelong learning.

weaknesses.point: Strong assumption that all tasks can be reasonably reformulated as QA without ablation
weaknesses.evidence: No analysis or examples on how complex structured prediction tasks are linearized or how this affects performance.

weaknesses.point: No quantitative evaluation of pseudo-sample quality or its impact on forgetting
weaknesses.evidence: The paper only qualitatively notes ‘chaos’ in generated samples without BLEU, perplexity, or correlation to forgetting.
No reported metrics on additional training time, memory consumption, or inference latency incurred by online pseudo-sample generation.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation scope with moderate-sized, mostly text-only datasets
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments are restricted to up to 9 sequential tasks on decaNLP and text classification, lacking large-scale or multimodal tasks.

weaknesses.point: Lack of analysis on scalability of vocabulary growth and embedding size due to task-specific tokens
weaknesses.evidence: No discussion on memory or speed implications when scaling to dozens of tasks.

weaknesses.point: No experiments or analysis on longer task sequences or dynamic sampling strategies
weaknesses.evidence: No evaluation beyond 9 tasks or investigation of curriculum/dynamic ratios for pseudo-sample mixing.

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility concerns due to missing hyperparameter details and generation procedures
weaknesses.evidence: Key details such as learning rates, beam widths, or sampling methods are omitted.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on failure modes and method limitations
weaknesses.evidence: Brief mention of noisy samples but no in-depth analysis of when LAMOL may be unsuitable.

questions.question: How does reformulating all tasks as QA affect performance on structured prediction tasks?
questions.motivation: Understanding this is crucial as the QA reformulation is central but may not fit all tasks naturally.

questions.question: How does the quality and diversity of generated pseudo-samples quantitatively correlate with forgetting mitigation?
questions.motivation: Quantitative metrics would clarify the role of pseudo-sample fidelity in lifelong learning success.

questions.question: What is the computational overhead (time, memory) of online pseudo-sample generation compared to standard replay methods?
questions.motivation: Assessing scalability and practical deployment requires understanding resource costs.

questions.question: Can LAMOL scale to 20+ tasks without excessive vocabulary growth or performance degradation?
questions.motivation: Lifelong learning demands scalability; without this, applicability is limited.

questions.question: Would dynamic or curriculum-based pseudo-sample mixing improve stability and final performance?
questions.motivation: Static sampling ratios may not be optimal; adaptive strategies could yield gains.

critique_points.point_001: Missing comparisons to recent generative replay methods in NLP. The paper lacks baselines using transformer-based generative replay or GPT-style replay frameworks relevant to lifelong learning
critique_points.point_002: Strong assumption that all tasks can be reasonably reformulated as QA without ablation. No analysis or examples on how complex structured prediction tasks are linearized or how this affects performance
critique_points.point_003: No quantitative evaluation of pseudo-sample quality or its impact on forgetting. The paper only qualitatively notes ‘chaos’ in generated samples without BLEU, perplexity, or correlation to forgetting
critique_points.point_004: Insufficient computational cost analysis. No reported metrics on additional training time, memory consumption, or inference latency incurred by online pseudo-sample generation
critique_points.point_005: Limited evaluation scope with moderate-sized, mostly text-only datasets. Experiments are restricted to up to 9 sequential tasks on decaNLP and text classification, lacking large-scale or multimodal tasks
critique_points.point_006: Lack of analysis on scalability of vocabulary growth and embedding size due to task-specific tokens. No discussion on memory or speed implications when scaling to dozens of tasks
critique_points.point_007: No experiments or analysis on longer task sequences or dynamic sampling strategies. No evaluation beyond 9 tasks or investigation of curriculum/dynamic ratios for pseudo-sample mixing
critique_points.point_008: Reproducibility concerns due to missing hyperparameter details and generation procedures. Key details such as learning rates, beam widths, or sampling methods are omitted
critique_points.point_009: Limited discussion on failure modes and method limitations. Brief mention of noisy samples but no in-depth analysis of when LAMOL may be unsuitable
